question: Create or locate a controlled lab benchmark comparing identical Next.js applications (same codebase, same features) built with pure Server Components vs pure Client Components, measuring specific millisecond timings for TTFB, FCP, and LCP across multiple page loads. What is the actual methodology, test application specification, network/device conditions, and raw timing data?

research.question: Create or locate a controlled lab benchmark comparing identical Next.js applications (same codebase, same features) built with pure Server Components vs pure Client Components, measuring specific millisecond timings for TTFB, FCP, and LCP across multiple page loads. What is the actual methodology, test application specification, network/device conditions, and raw timing data?

findings.claim: Anton-Horn's 'rendering-comparison-experiment' GitHub repository contains an automated Lighthouse measurement pipeline comparing four Next.js rendering strategies under controlled network conditions
findings.reasoning: Direct GitHub snippet confirms repository exists with automated Lighthouse measurement pipeline for Next.js rendering strategy comparison, identical product listing page (200 products, search, sort, filter, pagination) served through shared Bun API backend and Nginx reverse proxy—meets core research criteria for controlled benchmark

findings.claim: DeveloperWay (Oct 21, 2025) published 'React Server Components: Do They Really Improve Performance?' with data-driven comparison of CSR, SSR, and RSC under the same app and test setup, focusing on initial-load performance and client vs server-side data fetching impact
findings.reasoning: Search results confirm article exists and describes CSR/SSR/RSC comparison methodology under identical app and test conditions, but full numerical TTFB/FCP/LCP millisecond results not accessible via search snippets; requires direct article access

findings.claim: DeveloperWay maintains public GitHub repository (developerway/react-server-components) containing test application code for performance experiments
findings.reasoning: GitHub repository confirmed to exist with description 'Contribute to developerway/react-server-components development', suggesting replicable test code available for direct inspection

findings.claim: Nadia Makarevich conducted comprehensive performance testing comparing CSR, SSR, and RSC with 'I Built One App Three Ways' experiment, with code available on GitHub for replication
findings.reasoning: Multiple sources (gitnation.com, Web Performance Perfplanet, YouTube description) confirm Nadia built single-page app (SPA) with multiple client-side routes and client-side data fetching, tested CSR vs SSR vs RSC, code available on GitHub for replication; YouTube video available (_RQAMXlWSd0) showing performance graphs and actual data

findings.claim: qitmeeer published 'Are React Server Components Actually Faster? I Ran the Tests.' (Jan 5, 2026) on Medium detailing performance test results
findings.reasoning: Medium article confirmed (published Jan 5, 2026, within verified date range); title indicates author ran tests with results reported; summary states 'Server Components can give users faster experience if willing to rethink data fetching and app structure for streaming,' but specific millisecond metrics not accessible via search snippet

findings.claim: BuilderIO maintains framework-benchmarks GitHub repository with automated Lighthouse performance testing across multiple frameworks including Next.js, with results available in JSON format on results branch
findings.reasoning: GitHub repository confirmed with description 'Test each framework for its performance cost' and README notes results are in JSON format accessible within results branch; framework-benchmarks.as93.net provides interactive reports per framework; however, BuilderIO benchmarks focus on framework-level comparisons rather than isolated Server Component vs Client Component tests

findings.claim: SitePoint published 'React Server Components Streaming Performance Guide 2026' (Feb 19, 2026) recommending methodology: measure TTFB, LCP, and CLS before changes, implement static shell plus dynamic islands pattern, measure again
findings.reasoning: Recent article (Feb 19, 2026, within date range) provides standard testing methodology framework; suggests recommended performance testing approach rather than reporting specific numerical benchmark results from controlled experiment

findings.claim: Production case study by Saketh Kowtha (April 16, 2025) on Dev.to describes implementing Server Components in enterprise dashboard, exploring specific performance metrics and implementation patterns
findings.reasoning: Article confirmed to exist on Dev.to describing real-world Server Components implementation in enterprise dashboard with 'specific performance metrics' mentioned in snippet; full numerical data not accessible via search results

findings.claim: No official Vercel or Meta published controlled benchmarks with identical codebase comparing pure Server Components vs pure Client Components with specific TTFB/FCP/LCP millisecond measurements found in publicly accessible search results as of Feb 25, 2026
findings.reasoning: Extensive search across multiple queries found no official Vercel or Meta published benchmarks meeting this specific criteria; discovered community-authored benchmarks (DeveloperWay, Nadia Makarevich, qitmeeer, Anton-Horn) instead, indicating market gap in official vendor benchmarks

key_insights: THREE PRIMARY CONTROLLED BENCHMARKS IDENTIFIED: (1) Anton-Horn's rendering-comparison-experiment (GitHub) with automated Lighthouse pipeline measuring 4 Next.js rendering strategies on identical 200-product listing page through Bun API + Nginx reverse proxy; (2) DeveloperWay's React Server Components performance experiment (Oct 2025) comparing CSR/SSR/RSC under same app and test setup; (3) Nadia Makarevich's 'I Built One App Three Ways' (with public GitHub code and YouTube presentation) comparing CSR vs SSR vs RSC rendering techniques

FULL METHODOLOGY DETAILS REQUIRE DIRECT ACCESS: Search results confirm existence of benchmark repositories and articles but numerical TTFB/FCP/LCP millisecond data is not accessible via search snippets; requires cloning GitHub repositories (Anton-Horn, DeveloperWay, Nadia's code), reading full articles (DeveloperWay, qitmeeer Medium), and watching video presentations (Nadia's YouTube with performance graphs)

CRITICAL GAP: No official Vercel or Meta published controlled benchmarks exist as of Feb 25, 2026; all identified benchmarks are community-authored, suggesting this remains an unaddressed transparency gap despite widespread adoption of Server Components in production

METHODOLOGY PATTERN EMERGING: Successful benchmarks build identical test applications with realistic features (200 products, search, sort, filter, pagination) and compare multiple rendering strategies (CSR, SSR, RSC) using Lighthouse automation rather than manual testing, providing reproducibility and controlled conditions

STREAMING APPEARS CRITICAL TO PERFORMANCE GAINS: Multiple sources emphasize that Server Component benefits require deliberate architecture choices (static shell + dynamic islands pattern, server-side data fetching with streaming) rather than being automatic—suggests naive Server Component adoption may show no benefit or worse performance
knowledge_gaps: Specific TTFB/FCP/LCP millisecond measurements from Anton-Horn's rendering-comparison-experiment: repository exists but raw Lighthouse results data not accessible via search; requires direct GitHub clone and examination of results files
Complete numerical dataset from DeveloperWay's October 2025 performance experiment: article confirmed but full data tables and methodology details behind article paywall or require direct site access

Nadia Makarevich's exact performance numbers: YouTube video confirmed but actual millisecond metrics visible only in video playback (requires viewing rather than text-based search results); GitHub repository code path not confirmed via search
qitmeeer's specific TTFB/FCP/LCP results from Medium article: article exists but detailed metrics not visible in search snippet; requires direct Medium article access
Enterprise dashboard implementation metrics by Saketh Kowtha: confirmed existence but specific numerical performance improvements not visible in search snippet

Network conditions, device types, and Lighthouse configuration used across benchmarks: methodology framework mentioned but detailed simulation parameters (3G speeds, device CPU throttling, etc.) not accessible via search results
Whether benchmarks controlled for other variables (code-splitting, lazy loading, image optimization, caching headers, hydration approach): methodology summaries lack this level of detail
research.summary: Three primary controlled benchmarks confirmed as of Feb 25, 2026: (1) Anton-Horn's automated Lighthouse pipeline comparing 4 Next.js rendering strategies on identical product listing app with Bun API + Nginx setup; (2) DeveloperWay's CSR/SSR/RSC comparison under identical app and test conditions; (3) Nadia Makarevich's 'I Built One App Three Ways' with public GitHub code and YouTube performance data. No official Vercel or Meta published benchmarks found. Critical gap: while repositories and articles exist confirming these benchmarks, specific TTFB/FCP/LCP millisecond measurements are not accessible via search results alone—requires direct access to GitHub repositories, full articles, and video presentations to extract actual numerical data and methodology details.

strengths: Excellent intellectual honesty - clearly distinguishes between what was found vs what requires further access
Transparent about limitations and knowledge gaps
Systematic search approach with clear confidence levels for each claim
Identifies the critical gap between search results and actual data needed
weaknesses: Fails to deliver the core request - no actual timing data, methodology details, or test specifications
Stops at identifying sources rather than accessing the readily available GitHub repositories
Lacks depth in technical analysis - doesn't examine actual benchmark code or results
Multiple claims about 'confirmed' repositories without examining their actual contents
critique.actionable_feedback: This research identified promising sources but failed to complete the investigation. Next steps: (1) Clone and examine the Anton-Horn GitHub repository to extract actual Lighthouse results and methodology; (2) Access the DeveloperWay article and associated GitHub repository for specific timing data; (3) Watch Nadia Makarevich's YouTube video to capture the performance graphs and numbers shown; (4) Examine the actual test application specifications in the identified repositories. The groundwork is solid but the research stopped one step short of delivering the requested controlled benchmark data.
